responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Factors in the Development and Significance of Early Object Relationships11

Margaret Fries M.D.

Margaret Fries

Margaret Fries  

SUMMARY

Data on early psychic functioning were presented from two approaches:

1. From the analyses of adolescent and postadolescent patients whose archaic fantasies and earliest oral conflicts came to the fore in anticipation of marriage and parenthood. These analyses gave the opportunity to trace, in the same person, the influence of their earliest psychic functioning on their present ego ideals as mate and parent.

2. From observations from longitudinal studies; these, too, point to the importance of the interaction of neuromuscular maturity, environmental influences with congenital factors, such as the congenital activity type, protodefenses, and instinctual drives.

We raised the questions as to: (a) the relation of protodefenses to later ego defenses; (b) the possible importance for future erotism of early incidental autostimulation of erogenous zones and various parts of the body concomitant with the infantile startle; (c) the possible incorporation into the body image of the early body movements and of special areas stimulated; (d) whether the possibility of future psychotic behavior in infants with pathological congenital activity types can be reduced if treatment of their disturbed parents starts at birth.

All evidence from various sources converges on the importance of the mother's object relationship which enables her to be a suitable object for the infant's cathexis with whom he can identify; at the same time it helps him discharge tensions and bind his aggression.

responseSet.title: A Survey of Freud's Writings on Earliest Psychic Functioning
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Survey of Freud's Writings on Earliest Psychic Functioning11

David Rubinfine M.D.

David Rubinfine

David Rubinfine  

Assessment of the growing body of genetic and developmental hypotheses which constitute our theories of earliest psychic functioning through the end of the preverbal stage becomes ever more pressing. Mushrooming of unverifiable theories ultimately discredits our science by leading to unfounded innovations in technique. What is most needed is the development of creative research methods aimed at validation of theory rather than a further proliferation of hypotheses.

Unfortunately hypotheses concerning the earliest preverbal stages are not directly verifiable in our clinical work. An exciting beginning has been made in this direction through psychoanalytically oriented observations of infant (Fries, Spitz and Wolf; Kris et al.; Bergman, Escalona, and Leitch; Benjamin; Mittelmann).

A number of assumptions and hypotheses concerning this area appear in the writings of Sigmund Freud. Some of these are quite concisely stated, others are tacit and must be inferred (a pursuit that offers a rich yield), and still others are very fragmentary. Nowhere is there a systematic, comprehensive exposition of Freud's theories of earliest mental functioning. In attempting such a survey now, I shall deliberately limit myself to his theoretical constructs and formulations, and exclude the clinical experiences which suggested these hypotheses to him.

Submitted January 30, 1959

1Read at the Panel on Some Theoretical Aspects of Early Psychic Functioning at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December, 1958.2I wish to express appreciation to Dr. Charles Brenner, Dr. Milton Wexler, and especially to Dr. David Rapaport, for their most valuable criticisms and suggestions.610

[[RunningHead]]

A Note on Trauma and Loss

William Murphy M.D.

William Murphy

William Murphy  

SUMMARY

The need for a screen is of primary importance for the development of a trauma. The readiness to develop a traumatic state occurs following the summation of many fantasy traumata which are screens for old unresolved narcissistic injuries. These are reactivated by multiple narcissistic injuries in the recent present. Such

531

injuries are occasioned by losses of objects necessary for the gratification of instinctual aims, losses of ego ideals, and losses of ego control. A traumatic event externalizes an internal conflict and is related to acting out. It aids in the establishment of a phobic type of defense. It also bolsters the mechanisms of avoidance, denial, and dissociation, and protects against primary anxiety, severe ego disintegration, deep depression, and severe regressive states.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mirror Dreams11

Alan Eisnitz M.D.

Alan Eisnitz

Alan Eisnitz  

SUMMARY

1. The mirror dream represents an attempt at defense against narcissistic mortification from the superego, from the analyst, or from reality. Often it represents the defense against a castration threat resulting from oedipal conflicts centering around primal-scene fantasies.

2. In the case of the superego threat, the threatening part of the superego is split off and projected to the mirror.

3. Omnipotent voyeurism accomplishes the mastery of the projected imago; it can then be reintrojected safely as a protector of narcissism.

[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Dream of the Botanical Monograph11

Alexander Grinstein M.D.

Alexander Grinstein

Alexander Grinstein  

It is not without some hesitation that one undertakes a study of the early persona life of the founder of psychoanalysis. To a large extent this feeling is based upon Freud's own comments in The Interpretation of Dreams(4) where he stated:

The only dreams open to my choice were my own and those of my patients undergoing psycho-analytic treatment. … But if I was to report my own dreams, it inevitably followed that I should have to reveal to the public gaze more of the intimacies of my mental life than I liked, or than is normally necessary for any writer who is a man of science and not a poet. Such was the painful but unavoidable necessity; and I have submitted to it rather than totally abandon the possibility of giving the evidence for my psychological findings. Naturally, however, I have been unable to resist the temptation of taking the edge off some of my indiscretions by omissions and substitutions. But whenever this has happened, the value of my instances has been very definitely diminished. I can only express a hope that the readers of this book will put themselves in my difficult situation and treat me with indulgence, … [p. xxiii f.].

In Chapter 2 of the same work, Freud reiterates the difficulties which he had in the selection and presentation of personal material. He wrote:

There is some natural hesitation about revealing so many intimate facts about one's mental life; nor can there be any guarantee against misinterpretation by strangers. But it must

responseSet.title: Blank Silence and the Dream Screen
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Blank Silence and the Dream Screen

Carel Van Der Heide M.D.

Carel Van Der Heide

Carel Van Der Heide  

It is common knowledge that silence may bring people together. Collective prayers, memorial commemorations, the silent minutes of the Oxford movement, and other rituals, in part, seem to serve this function.

In the analytic situation, speech or intelligible speech at times is abolished. A patient of Mary O'Neil Hawkins (5) resorted to prolonged mumbling when he perceived the analyst as his mother whom, by death, he had lost early in life. During these episodes he assumed that the analyst saw the same pictures he saw, which represented ideas "unassociated with words." However, the experience of a shared, mutual silence occasionally outweighs in importance the ideational content. An analytic patient once was aware of this when she said: "Let us be silent about something else."

In psychoanalysis, passing silences often are caused by transference resistance. Epitomizing the operational importance of transference in psychoanalysis, and transference resistance in relation to speech, Karl Landauer once said that the analysis really does not move until the talkative patient becomes silent, to which he pointedly added: "and the silent patient starts talking."

Departing from our discussion of the chronically silent patient in psychoanalysis, I should like to speak of a "blank silence, " a special form of transient, often recurrent cessation of speech. It is perhaps best defined as a temporary, functional ego regression, defensive in nature and indicative of a considerable alteration of the dominant object relation.

Submitted June 10, 1959

85

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Empathy and the Bridge Function of the Ego

Antonio Ferreira M.D.

Antonio Ferreira

Antonio Ferreira  

SUMMARY

The phenomenon of empathy is discussed in terms of psychoanalytic theory with a view to trace its origin, nature, and vicissitudes as they relate to the development of the psychic apparatus. Empathy is rooted in a primary unity with mother, and its fate and course relate to the development of the ego. It becomes eventually organized into an ego function—a bridge function of

103

the ego—that subserves the striving toward contact with the nonself. The problem of empathy versus projection is illustrated by a discussion of the schizophrenic's "sensitivity." A word of caution is said regarding the validity and interpretation of the so-called empathy tests.

Book Notices

AN APPLICATION OF PSYCHOANALYSIS TO EDUCATION. By Richard M. Jones. Introduction by Lawrence S. Kubie [Publication Number 384, American Lecture Series. A Monograph in American lectures in Psychology, edited by Millie Harrower ]. Springfield, Ill.: Charles C Thomas, 1960, xi + 124 pp., $5.50.

The project described in this book, consisting of a piece of "action research" (K. Lewin) on ethnic prejudice, was conducted as a "self-knowledge" workshop at a traditional New England preparatory school, with two control groups, one from the same school, and the other from another of more liberal nature. The assumption that new mores are required for our schools, which, in the words of Lawrence Kubie's Introduction, "will enable young people from early years to understand and feel and put into words all the hidden things which go on in them, " but which "does not carry with it the right to act out … feelings blindly, " is an integral part of the pedagogic philosophy of the author of the book and his co-workers. The author, who is familiar with psychoanalytic theory and practice and thoroughly sympathetic to it, was aided by Kubie, Leo Berman, Elvin Semrad, and Gordon Allport. His experimental design is clear and detailed and reflects expert psychological and psychoanalytic knowledge. The basic attempt was to determine whether a workshop in self-knowledge would increase self-acceptance and result in a reduction in race prejudice. Although the author states that "The workshop worked no miracles … no neurotics were cured, nor was anyone's future happiness assured … no bigots were transformed, " there was "a measurably significant increase in attitudes of self-acceptance, " and there was, as an indirect effect, a reduction in race prejudice.

BASIC THEORY OF PSYCHOANALYSIS. By Robert Waelder. New York: International Universities Press, 1960, xiv + 273 pp., $5.00.

Unlike Rapaport's essay, The Structure of Psychoanalytic Theory [Psychological Issues, Vol. II, No. 2, Mon. No. 6, 1960], this series of essays does not attempt to treat the more abstract aspects of psychoanalytic theory in any but the most cursory fashion. Waelder deals "only with what [he considers] to be basic theory —its rationale, its implications, and the questions which it stimulates us to ask.

[[RunningHead]]

Maxwell Gitelson—A Tribute

When the Journal was organized, it was deemed advisable to establish the position of Chairman of the Editorial Board. For this appointment a most felicitous choice was made in the person of Max Gitelson.

The earlier period of the Journal was one of Sturm und Drang. None of the original members of the Board had had extensive experience in establishing and maintaining a journal of this type. It was a matter of urgency to promote mutual understanding among the participants. It is in no small measure due to Max Gitelson that this was achieved, and today the work of the Journal is carried on in a spirit of genuine harmony. By virtue of his personality, his patience, and his unfailing dedication to principle, he brought to bear a markedly beneficial influence on the development and growth of the Journal.

Even as the years went on, and it became increasingly evident that the original purpose for which the Chairmanship of the Board was established had long since been accomplished, Max Gitelson continued to make invaluable contributions to the maturation of the Journal. With his expressed wish to resign after this year as Chairman, this position is now being discontinued. Perhaps this is as it should be, since the Board Chairmanship has become uniquely associated with the name of Max Gitelson. He has eminently earned the lasting gratitude of the Association for his many years of devoted service to the Journal.

1

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Significance of Visual Images During the Analytic Session

Max Warren M.D.

Max Warren

Max Warren  

SUMMARY

In this paper, an attempt was made to re-examine the significance and technical use of the visual image in the analytic session as an aid in overcoming resistances. The visual images are elicited during an interruption in the patient's verbalizations. In some of the patients, after an initial inquiry as to visual imagery during a period of silence, subsequent visual elements were spontaneously described. Freud stated (9, pp. 36-37): "We make our observations through the medium of the same perceptual apparatus, precisely by the help of the breaks in the series of [conscious] mental events, since we fill in the omissions by plausible inferences and translate them into conscious material."

I believe that visual images enable us to be more certain of the plausibility of the inferences. Such images differ from dreams in that the plastic representation is usually more evident, and the relation to preceding material is more difficult to deny. As a rule, there is less distortion than one may see in dreams. In addition, the interval between the image and the patient's talking about it minimizes the time available for secondary elaboration.

The visual image in lieu of verbalization, in cases I have seen, seems to occur in patients having pronounced sadomasochistic

517

character structures who are highly narcissistic, experience repetitive humiliation fantasies, and express a great need for love and approval.

Verbalization is more object directed than visual imagery. Regression to visual imagery is a more narcissistically cathected representation satisfying id and superego drives. When the relationship to the analyst is made tenuous by the emergence of derivatives of anxiety-producing drives in the transference, the visual image may be seen as having an economic function of discharging impulses which the patient fears might disturb the wished-for relationship with the analyst.

[[RunningHead]]

Panel Reports—Resistances in Child Analysis11

Marjorie Harley Ph.D.

Marjorie Harley

Marjorie Harley  

In his opening remarks, Gregory Rochlin stated that while we are prone to give sweeping emphasis to the distinctions between the resistances of the child in analysis and those of the adult, we have not been specific in defining these distinctions. He further suggested that specific types of resistance may bear a relation to specific maturational phases, and that this relation may be particularly important in delineating the nature of transference in various age groups. Of importance, also, is to what degree the transference is internalized, and to what degree it is fluid.

Elsie L. Haug then presented a paper on "Some Developmental Aspects of Resistance in Children, " with special emphasis on the issue of resistance in the transference neurosis and of the transference neurosis. In Haug's terms, an internal conflict is the result of the internalization of the external conflict the child has experienced with his mother in respect to her attitudes toward his instinctual strivings. When internal forces threaten the emergence of the repressed, defense is used to inactivate these; when the analyst attempts to lift the repression, resistance is employed to inactivate him. In a phase of resistance, the internal conflict becomes unconscious and reappears in the form of an interpersonal conflict between the patient and the analyst. This transformation of internal conflicts into interpersonal ones is frequently employed by children, not only in their relationship to the analyst, but to their parents as well.

Haug traced the development of the capacity to substitute interpersonal conflict for internalized conflict. Although making the point that this process is active in all libidinal phases, Haug chose the anal phase for elaboration of her theme. On the one hand, the compliant behavior of the child at this time makes for interpersonal harmony, but a lessening of instinctual pleasure;

[[RunningHead]]

Aggression and Symptom Formation11

Samuel Lipton M.D.

Samuel Lipton

Samuel Lipton  

To aid the reader in orienting himself to this summary it may be useful if some prefatory remarks are presented concerning the difficulty the reporter experienced in preparing it and the formulations he found useful in conceptualizing this difficulty. The difficulty seemed to lie in the fact that there were areas of terminological and to some extent theoretical divergence that remained implicit and unsettled and left the various contributions, which in themselves were most interesting and stimulating, unrelated to a unified theme.

In the first place, there were data from classical analysis, from analyses of psychotic adults, from treatment of psychotic children, from observations of infants, and from studies of psychosomatic illness. In these diverse situations there are both major and subtle shifts in underlying assumptions. It is evident that when the term aggression is used in these varied situations its implications are often divergent.

Secondly, the term aggression is commonly used to mean several different concepts. (a) It is used to describe a form of psychic energy, as contrasted with libido. In this context are described intrapsychic cathectic changes such as the investment of an object representation with aggressive energy. (b) It is used to describe ideas or behavior, or both, which are angry, hateful, or destructive. Thus, striking someone may be called aggression. (c) The term is used to describe action or activity. These distinctions are usually easy to discern according to the context. More difficult is the situation when observable behavior is considered aggression and is conceived of as coinciding with and accurately representing corresponding cathectic changes. This direct conclusion contrasts with conceptualizing the indirect repercussions of deduced cathectic changes.

For example, if an object representation is cathected with aggressive energy and the person therefore destroys the actual object in external reality, there is such a close correspondence between intrapsychic cathectic changes and behavior that the distinction seems academic. Yet the investment of an object representation with aggression may destroy the representation, damage the ego boundary, and leave the real external object unscathed.

[[RunningHead]]

The Last Hour11

Samuel Lipton M.D.

Samuel Lipton

Samuel Lipton  

While the principles of psychoanalytic technique are well known, they are constantly subject to re-examination and reevaluation. The need for such re-examination comes from theoretical modifications, broadened therapeutic demands, and other sources, and is a matter of consistent interest. Concurrently, our work provides us with material that validates well-known elements of our procedure. This material is also worth noting because there is a tendency for the old to become the archaic. It is therefore the purpose of this essay to record an especially clear illustration of the value of a particular technical point.

The point itself is well known. It is that the analytic process is continued to the conclusion of the analysis. Various authors use different terms to describe this idea, but there is general agreement. To cite one particularly succinct example, Glover, in his book on technique, writes that the association rule remains in force to the last minute of the last hour.

We might ask why the matter should be emphasized or even made explicit or ask what alternative we have. In part the answer comes from our patients who so frequently look forward to some form of transference gratification at the conclusion and tend to use this period as an important enclave for fantasies. One of my patients said, "I have hoped that during the last week you would answer all my questions." Others have expressed the wish to sit up. In several such instances the analysis of the latent fantasy proved to be valuable. The conclusion of an analysis also has its impact on the analyst who at that time may tend toward accentuating the interpersonal relationship because of his own needs.

Submitted January 18, 1960

1Presented at the Brief Communications Section of the Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association on December 4, 1959, and emended after discussions by Drs. Phyllis Greenacre and Grace De Bell.325

Heinz Lichtenstein M.D.

GENERAL BIOLOGICAL CONSIDERATIONS CONCERNING THE ROLE OF NONPROCREATIVE SEXUALITY IN MAN

In his Outline of Psychoanalysis Freud (63), (64) summarizes the main results of psychoanalytic research concerning the sexual function as follows:

a. The sexual function does not start with puberty, but begins soon after birth with clear manifestations.

b. It is necessary to make a sharp distinction between the concepts sexual and genital. The first concept is wider and includes many activities which have nothing to do with the genitals.

c. Sexuality is defined as the function of gaining pleasurable gratification from specific bodily zones. This function is placed in the service of procreation only secondarily. Often both functions do not completely fuse [tr. by author].

Since Freud first postulated these three fundamental theses regarding sexuality in his Three Contributions(52)

responseSet.title: The Psychology of Silence—Its Role in Transference, Countertransference and the Psychoanalytic Process
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychology of Silence—Its Role in Transference, Countertransference and the Psychoanalytic Process

Meyer Zeligs M.D.

Meyer Zeligs

Meyer Zeligs  

SUMMARY

Silence is a complex psychic state not easily classified or systematized. Though antithetical to speech on a verbal level, silence communicates nonverbally an existing mood, attitude, aggressive or libidinous thought or feeling.

The closely interrelated meaning and interchanging uses of silence, verbalization, and body posture, as observed during the analytic hour, have been emphasized and clinically illustrated as modes of verbal and nonverbal communication.

From the structural viewpoint of psychic function, silence may be conceived as emanating from any of the systems or intersystemic processes of the psychic apparatus. Silence may derive from id, ego, superego, or be interposed in the systems id-ego, id-superego, ego-superego. In the light of present-day knowledge of transference and countertransference, the role which the patient's silence and the analyst's silence play in the psychoanalytic process has been more closely investigated. I have tried to show how the quality of feelings emanating from the analyst, during silence as well as when he speaks, provides the matrix in which the analytic process is initiated. This may impede or promote the analytic process. As the analysis progresses and the infantile part of the patient's ego undergoes maturation, the initially fragile interpersonal relationship gradually becomes more structured, fortified, and objectivized. This early (preverbal) phase of object relations is progressively strengthened by the empathic content of the analyst's silences and verbalizations and ultimately made meaningful by his interpretations. Throughout the course of treatment this interpersonal relationship persists as a delicate, operational framework, at times gratifying and at times frustrating. The affective content immanent in the analytic situation, the empathic quality of which stems from pregenital levels and primary object relations, is a genetic determinant of the nonverbal and verbal nature of transference and countertransference.

During the course of analysis, patients re-enact all or many of

41

the original uses and meanings of silence which have accumulated or been synthesized from different developmental levels. Unless the analyst is aware of the many characteristic meanings and uses of the patient's and of his own silences, he too will unconsciously re-employ his original silences. With a broadened conceptualization of the metapsychology of silence and an increased technical awareness of its continuous role in transference and countertransference, this complex, sometimes vexing, but always-to-be-reckoned-with human attitude can be brought into clearer focus as a part of the psychoanalytic process.

[[RunningHead]]

The Analyst and the Hippocratic Oath11

James McLaughlin M.D.

James McLaughlin

James McLaughlin  

SUMMARY

The behavior of the analyst is importantly affected by his identity as physician, by his need to be a healer. Our cultural image of the physician is shaped by the unchanging imperatives of the sick, epitomized from the past in the Oath of Hippocrates and in the present by the stereotype of "The Old Family Doctor." The qualities of compassion and self-sacrifice constant to these idealized images are also demonstrable in the classic model of the analyst. Their presence implies extreme renunciation of instinctual gratification, except in the altered form of service for others. Examination of the myth and legend of Aesculapius reveals dynamic themes clearly pertinent to the renunciations of the Hippocratic Oath. The major themes include avoidance of open oedipal identification and rivalry with the aggressive rapacity of the father; avoidance of rage at the deserting mother and rival siblings; sublimative or reactive alteration of these destructive and erotic strivings into a serving, preserving, and healing of others that constitutes a reparative identification with the nurturing mother, an effort to please her and placate the father; re-emergence of the aggressive competitiveness as efforts to usurp the paternal prerogatives of knowledge and benevolent conquest disguised as service to others; eventual destruction for trespassing,

122

even in this fashion, too far into the paternal domain of knowledge and discovery. Similar patterns of conflict and resolution are demonstrated in the personal dynamics and professional conduct of physician patients and training analysands in whom the commitment to healing is great. Common tendencies to masochistic enactment and intellectual inhibition can be traced to these dynamics, and often go unanalyzed because of their ego-syntonic presence in the cultural and ego ideal of the good physician.

[[RunningHead]]

Ego Psychology and the Study of Mythology11

Jacob Arlow M.D.

Jacob Arlow

Jacob Arlow  

Descending like a meteor from the heavens, then wafted gently earthward on the streams of the terrestrial atmosphere, in a boat especially fashioned to protect him from the terrors of the sun-filled darkness of outer space, the first cosmonaut of history emerged from his capsule. In less than twenty-four hours, at a speed incomparably greater than any ever ascribed to the winged Hermes, messenger of the Gods, there raced around the world the echoes of an ancient story. This hero, the son of a Soviet carpenter, the report said, was actually descended from distinguished nobility. Within the day, the mythopoetic function of mankind had already embraced the first hero of the space age.

Joseph Campbell (11) writes:

Man apparently cannot maintain himself in the universe without belief in some arrangement of the general inheritance of myth. In fact, the fullness of his life would even seem to stand in direct ratio to the depth and range not of his rational thought, but of his local mythology. Whence the force of these insubstantial themes, by which they are empowered to galvanize populations, creating of them civilizations, each with a beauty and self-compelling destiny of its own? And why should it be that whenever men have looked for something solid on which to found their lives, they have chosen not the facts in which the world abounds, but the myths of an immemorial imagination—preferring even to make life a hell for themselves and their neighbors, in the name of some violent God, rather than to accept gracefully the bounty the world affords?

From Freud on, psychoanalysts have studied the compelling, unknown, and ofttimes irrational forces of the human mind.

Submitted May 20, 1961

1Presidential Address, presented before the American Psychoanalytic Association, Chicago, May 7, 1961.371

SUMMARY

The application of a procedure for the evaluation of the results of analysis is demonstrated. A satisfactorily analyzed patient was seen by a second analyst four years after the termination of analysis in a series of once-a-week interviews. responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Aspects of Ego Vicissitudes in Adolescence

Elisabeth Geleerd M.D.

Elisabeth Geleerd

Elisabeth Geleerd  

SUMMARY

A few factors of influence on ego development in adolescence have been discussed.

It is suggested that in adolescence a partial regression to the undifferentiated phase of object relationship occurs. This explains the increased need for a love object and the need for union with this love object. The need may find direct expression, or it may manifest itself in dedication to some cause or other. But it is always accompanied by the fear that the regression may go too far and result in dissolution of the self. This fear explains much of the adolescent's defiant and rebellious behavior, and his sudden, unpredictable changes of love object.

Along with the partial regression, there is an increase in perceptiveness, directed both inwardly, as a result of awareness of the many bodily changes, and toward the outside world. This increased

404

perceptiveness explains the enthusiasm, the capacity for being inspired, that is sublimated in many cases in artistic or intellectual endeavors, although it may also be felt as an unfulfilled longing.

Due to the vulnerability of the ego in adolescence, real life traumata have a serious impact and lead in most cases to neurosis in adult life.

It should not be forgotten, however, that in spite of all the turmoil, adolescence is the period of life in which there occurs the most extensive growth and maturation in preparation for adulthood. I would like to suggest that although it may lead to pathological reactions, partial regression to the undifferentiated phase is a physiological vicissitude of the ego necessary for the full process of growth and maturation.

[[RunningHead]]

Some Observations on the Relationship Between Genitality and Structural Development at Adolescence11

Marjorie Harley Ph.D.

Marjorie Harley

Marjorie Harley  

SUMMARY

In this oversimplified and sketchy presentation of an adolescent boy's history and analysis, I have tried to show how the failure of previous maturational phases so weakened the genital organization at adolescence as to rob the ego of a powerful stimulus toward its own change and growth; and how, as genitality became strengthened, this change and growth did begin to take place. In general, the structural dissolutions which take place during adolescence involve structures which have been built up throughout childhood and have been rather firmly consolidated by the end of the latency period. However, when previous maturational phases, particularly that of the phallic-oedipal, have failed as atypically as they did in this boy, the process of dissolution may be largely limited to pregenital psychic structures and followed more by a deferred structural development than by a structural reorganization. Although this process quite probably would not take place without the aid of analysis, it is greatly facilitated by the maturational forces which work toward a shift in drive organization.

There is a certain amount of disagreement in regard both to the advisability as well as the efficacy of attempting analysis with

458

adolescents, because certain aspects of the maturational changes of this period may work against rather than for the treatment. With this boy, adolescence seemed a propitious time for analysis. This, I believe, may be an important consideration, since it carries the implication that when previous development has predisposed toward a weak genital organization, the physiological changes of puberty, in some such instances, may favor the analysis, and provide the analyst with a valuable opportunity to help the ego avoid a fixed, regressive adaptation. To put this in my patient's terms, although he had never completely abandoned his underlying wish to regain the "spirit" of his penis, it was puberty that supplied him with sufficient impetus to seek to claim it in and through himself.

I have not attempted to discuss in what measure the latency and prepubertal periods of treatment contributed to the course of the adolescent phase of this boy's analysis, a factor which should not be overlooked. The question of what final form his ego-libidinal development will assume in adulthood must of necessity await a future answer.

[[RunningHead]]

Panel Reports—Learning Difficulties in Childhood11

E. Anthony M.D.

E. Anthony

E. Anthony  

In opening the session, Irene Josselyn referred to the title of the panel as "all-encompassing" and wondered what limitations she should impose on the deliberations. She concluded that since learning and difficulties both had infinite ramifications within the process of psychological growth, there was nothing that could really be done about it (the discussion was, at least, confined to the period of childhood).

Josselyn approached learning in its widest connotation, tracing it from its earliest condition or sensorimotor forms. She next asked the pertinent question as to whether the stimulus-tension-discharge model of psychoanalysis had any part to play in learning theory. It would be theoretically parsimonious to be able to include both emotional and cognitive aspects within a single theory of dynamic adaptation. She seemed generally concerned that we should keep our feet firmly on biological ground and that we should do our thinking against the background of such fundamental physical data as the corticalsubcortical systems, nervous system maturation, and experimental observations of early neonatal behavior. (This served a sobering purpose, since learning is a theme that can quite easily take flight into the higher realms of epistemology, although I myself see no great disadvantage in this; in fact, I look forward to the day when we can establish a dynamic, genetic epistemology, demonstrating the growth of knowledge both at the preconscious and the conscious levels and the feeding by unconscious sources.) Fortunately, the maturation versus learning problem did not raise its controversial head, although this is the year in which Gesell's eightieth birthday is being celebrated, and without this to complicate matters, the panel was able to cover a large section of the ambitious intention to survey the existent literature and then to consider the clinical problems raised by learning difficulties at different stages of development. Upon a biological substratum, Josselyn laid a basic psychological layer—the multiphasic structure of memory, recall, and of the "automatic utilization of prelearned material" which was at the core, she felt, of the learning experience.

[[RunningHead]]

The Role of Early Psychic Functioning in Psychoanalysis11

Leo Rangell M.D.

Leo Rangell

Leo Rangell  

This panel will be devoted to a consideration of some theoretical aspects of early psychic functioning, a subject to various individual components of which a considerable segment of literature has been directed in recent years. Recent panels at the American Psychoanalytic Association, for example, on The Psychoanalytic Theory of Affects (53), on The Theory of Thinking (3), and a symposium on Anxiety (75), have necessarily led back to the earliest origins of these psychic phenomena. Other discussions concerning early ego functioning, and most recently another panel which considered the origins of superego function (37), also led back of necessity to this earliest phase of life. Moreover, many writings which start out from quite other directions also point inevitably back to this period. For example, studies of borderline or psychotic patients (55), (63), a discussion of the pregenital elements in neurogenesis (64), direct child observations in both health and disease, as well as many specific studies of discrete subjects such as, to cite but one example, when Jacobson (33), (34), (35) attempts to sharpen the concept of the self, all lead to a quest for explanations and origins in the earliest phases of life. Having participated in a number of these separate discussions, it was conceived as advantageous to attempt to amalgamate, if possible, all these individual streams for a unified discussion of some common factors and considerations.

Submitted September 2, 1959

Also presented subsequently to the Los Angeles Society for Child Psychiatry, Los Angeles, California, February 26, 1959.595

Reconstruction and Theory Formation11

Rudolf Ekstein Ph.D. and Leo Rangell M.D.

Leo Rangell

Leo Rangell  

Freud frequently compared the work of the psychoanalyst with the excavations of the archaeologist, and demonstrated a variety of identical, or rather similar, features in the work of both. This comparison led him to suggest, in his late paper on "Constructions in Analysis" (9), how psychoanalytic interpretative work leads to the formation of constructions, a term which is more usually referred to in present-day literature as reconstruction. In this connection, he states that it

… is not, however, a preliminary labour in the sense that the whole of it must be completed before the next piece of work can be begun, as, for instance, is the case with house-building, where all the walls must be erected and all the windows inserted before the internal decoration of the rooms can be taken in hand. Every analyst knows that things happen differently in an analytic treatment and that there are both kinds of work carried on side by side, the one kind being always a little ahead and the other following upon it. The analyst finishes a piece of construction and communicates it to the subject of the analysis, so that it may work upon him; he then constructs a further piece out of the fresh material pouring in upon him, deals with it in the same way and proceeds in this alternating fashion until the end. If, in accounts of analytic technique, so little is said about 'constructions, ' that is because 'interpretations' and their effects are spoken of instead. But I think 'construction' is by far the more appropriate description. responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Adolescent Into Adult: An Inquiry11

Gerard Fountain M.D.

Gerard Fountain

Gerard Fountain  

SUMMARY

Adolescents differ from adults in many ways. We may select five characteristics not commonly prominent in adults but often seen in adolescents, at least those of a certain social class. They are among the qualities that make adolescence seem so strange to us, so compounded of extremes, so curiously unrealistic. Taken together, these qualities add up to impairment of synthetic function of the ego, reversion to domination by the pleasure principle, and a defect in contact with the outside world. The five qualities can be explained as due to increased narcissistic cathexis and regression to earlier stages of development. The present discussion, however, is confined to other factors that help to account for them.

The intensity and changeableness of adolescent feeling, and the seeking after it, are in part caused by the newness of the instinctual forces, the inexperience of the ego in using its resources, and the importance of emotional encounters if the ego is to acquire greater efficiency.

The immediacy of the adolescent's need for gratification, his inability to tolerate frustration, is partly at least caused by the fact that frustration represents castration—or abandonment or engulfment or other misfortune, depending upon the preoedipal experiences—and castration fears are heightened anyway in early adolescence, so that frustration adds an intolerable burden of anxiety.

The three other qualities may be attributed, again only in part, to the intense involvement of the adolescent in his oedipal fantasy. It is only as this oedipal fantasy can become more inclusive, can involve more objects of more kinds (among whom the oedipal roles can, as it were, be divided) that the adolescent, now becoming an adult, can appraise with accuracy his own behavior

432

and that of others and can begin to live as if he is a member of the world and not merely of a small corner of it.

[[RunningHead]]

Some Notes on English Freudian Terminology

Lewis Brandt

Lewis Brandt

Lewis Brandt  

Ernest Jones wrote in his autobiography: "… the translations in question were not only seriously inaccurate, with misunderstandings of the German text and ambiguous renderings that greatly impaired their value for scientific purposes, but were unworthy of Freud's style and gave a misleading impression of his personality" (11). Other psychoanalytic writers have complained about specific English psychoanalytic terms which do not render the German idea adequately. Thus, e.g., Eduardo Weiss remarked: "Freud's term … conveys a clear figurative representation … which has unfortunately been lost in the English translation" (16, p. 188).

Freud who "had a reputation as a master of German prose so high that he was even awarded the Goethe Prize at Frankfurt for his literary merits" (11) was extremely language conscious as evidenced by his Psychopathology in Everyday Life, his Interpretation of Dreams, and his use of free association. He had a good command of Latin and Greek and lived in Austria where words derived from the classical languages are used more frequently than in Prussia. He made use of such words (e.g., Penis, Telepathie) in many instances. Hence it may be assumed that whenever Freud used a common German word to name some psychological concept he was fully aware of all the connotations of that word and wanted its "affective elements, " as Bally (1) calls them, to enter into his use of the term. He must also have been aware of the fact that a common German word contains and conveys much more emotional immediacy than a new one formed or taken from a dead language.

Note: Wherever the reference is to a German edition, the translation of the quotation in this paper is mine. L.W.B.

Submitted March 7, 1960

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Observations on the Role of the Scrotal Sac and Testicles11

Anita Bell M.D.

Anita Bell

Anita Bell  

SUMMARY

We have indicated that particular characteristics of the testicles, i.e., their motility and ability to disappear completely, set them apart from other parts of the body including the penis. The influence of this phenomenon on the psychological development of the male, in my opinion, is far reaching.

We have noted through direct observations and analytic material that during the pregenital period the testicles and scrotal sac play a precursor role to that of the phallus in the development of castration anxiety. We know that phallic primacy begins to take over at about the middle of the third year and seems to obscure the role of testicles and scrotal sac. However, the pathway of castration anxiety still goes over the road of breast, feces, testicle, phallus. Each in turn provokes fear of loss of the object in the developing child. The simultaneous percept of the outline of the testicles and the dropping fecal mass during evacuation relates testicles to feces and hence to fear of object loss.

The contributing action of the cremasteric reflex, which can, and in most young boys does, cause a transitory upward movement and sometimes complete disappearance of the testicles, may serve

284

to intensify this fear during evacuation. Since the testicles can be drawn up into the inguinal canal, during the formative years, in moments of fear, and since they become exquisitely sensitive to pain when the boy matures, I believe that phallic castration anxiety is a composite of previously experienced oral, anal, and testicular anxiety as well as anxiety referable to the phallus.

From our description of the role of the testicles and scrotal sac, it seems to me that they have the characteristic of an erogenous zone lying between anus and phallus. The sequence of development in the male is such that it suggests the possibility of phase specificity in considering the role of the testicles and scrotal sac. I should like to propose consideration of the concept of a testicular phase in psychosexual development; also the possibility that fixation at, or regression to, this phase may take place.

In the development of the pattern and structure of feminine identification we have indicated that embryologically, biologically, and psychologically, the testicles and scrotal sac have been observed to have a feminine connotation. Because they present a visual image of paired appendages, they have been equated with breasts, ovaries, labia majora, and other organs occurring in pairs. The rear anatomical view of the male is not unlike a female genital. (This concept we have found expressed by some analytic patients, and by children during their pregenital phase.)

In so far as the testicles and scrotal sac connote femininity, and femininity connotes castration, any threat of eruption of such fantasies into consciousness will be vehemently guarded against, particularly during puberty, when the testicles enlarge and become sensitive to pain, and secondary sex characteristics appear. The mechanisms of choice, as gleaned from case material, seem to be repression, denial, and displacement to the penis.

The Significance of the Sense of Smell in the Transference11

Jean Rosenbaum M.D.

Jean Rosenbaum  

The purpose of this paper is to demonstrate that there are genital aspects to active and passive expressions of olfactory experiences encountered in oedipal transference material in therapy. An attempt will be made to show that acuity of the sense of smell may increase or decrease, appear or disappear, genetically and in treatment in connection with genital impulses and conflicts. An explanation of these smell expressions in therapy and genetically as either an autonomous ego function or as a manifestation of certain defensive activities within the ego will also be offered. The use of active or passive in this paper in connection with smell experiences refers to the subject smelling an object or the subject being smelled by an object, respectively.

In a letter to Fliess Freud discussed his theory of organic repression and the relation between man's susceptibility to nervous disease and his diminished acuity of smell. "To put it crudely, the current memory stinks just as an actual object may stink; and just as we turn away our sense organ (the head and nose) in disgust, so do the preconscious and our conscious apprehension turn away from memory. This is repression " (7, p. 232). Later he advanced some further phylogenetic and biogenetic speculations about the importance of man's diminished smell acuity as compared with that of lower vertebrates. He attributed the less pronounced development of smell to man's upright position (9), (10). responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Disorder and Consolidation in Adolescence11

Leo Spiegel M.D.

Leo Spiegel

Leo Spiegel  

Analytic and social observation of adolescents over an extended period of time permit one to see alternating phases of chaos and consolidation. As the end of adolescence approaches, the wild swings of mood and interest, the geniuslike insights into personal and social problems, the egotism and altruism, the overpowering drives simmer down to respectable levels of intensity which now make the adolescent a comfortable person to deal with, either socially or in our particular sphere, analytically.

In some ways, this favorable and normal solution makes the impression of a reduction from multipotential to unipotential forces. Of the many possibilities of personality and intellectual outcomes only one may be partially realized. Here maturation consists in reduction of possibilities; to speak with Hartmann, in analysis (as well as outside of it) we assist at the "murder of talents."2

Behind this screen of order and disorder, of chaos and consolidation, lie a number of processes which we invoke more or less loosely, in order to perceive structure in the shifting scene of adolescence. These processes consist in the struggle to achieve genitality, the struggle to attain a nonincestuous object, the struggle to achieve a firm sense of self, and in the struggle to achieve a steady relation between the three psychic systems. These processes interact with and rebound on each other, producing multitudinous clinical echoes in ways too complicated to trace fully in a given adolescent.

Submitted June 30, 1959

1From the Albert Einstein College of Medicine. Presented at the Fall Meeting, December, 1958, of the American Psychoanalytic Association, as part of a Panel on "The Vicissitudes of Ego Development in Adolescence."2Personal communication.406

Some Ego Considerations in the Silent Patient

Leo Loomie M.D.

Leo Loomie

Leo Loomie  

Today's discussion of the silent patient returns to a topic which has engaged analysts' attention repeatedly in the past. Both in 1927 and 1935, it was discussed in the Vienna Psychoanalytic Society, and the Hungarian Society considered the subject in 1934. Since we do not yet have a fully formulated understanding of this problem, nor very specific principles for the management of such cases, it is appropriate that we survey again a familiar terrain. This paper will review the literature and then offer some clinical experiences with silent patients.

REVIEW OF THE LITERATURE

In reporting on previous analytic research on this topic, no effort will be made fully to present the ideas of the authors selected. Rather it is proposed to excerpt certain highlights of the principal contributions in order to reflect how analytic thinking on the silent patient has broadened and to convey some impression of the multiple angles from which the subject has been considered.

One has only to advert to the essential role of speech in psychoanalytic therapy, to imagine that interruptions in this process would early engage attention. Ferenczi (5) in 1911 first wrote how the fear of uttering obscene words produced prolonged silence, while Freud (11) in 1912 identified transference difficulties as a cause of a breakdown of associations. In 1913, Ferenczi (6) discussed patients for whom there appears to be an equation of speech with action with an inevitable inhibiting consequence on verbalization. Freud (12)

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Masturbation and Character

Steven Hammerman M.D.

Steven Hammerman

Steven Hammerman  

SUMMARY

As the ego develops the capacity to distinguish between self-representations and object representations, narcissistic frustrations impede the development of real object relations. There result attempts to obtain instinctual gratification through various autoerotic activities with fantasies embodying excessive cathexis of the self-representation and the early object representations. These archaic fantasies become attached to the earliest rhythmic precursors of genital masturbation. Concomitant with the development of these earliest fantasies to their more complex derivatives, there occurs the development of the masturbation process up to the eventual act of genital masturbation or its various equivalents. Such genital masturbation or masturbation equivalents may be used to express pregenital conflicts related to any of the stages of psychosexual development through the associated masturbation fantasies. Derivatives of such unconscious infantile masturbation fantasies may also be lived out in behavior patterns, which may be isolated from conscious awareness and well integrated into the personality. Such basic unconscious fantasies with the excessive cathexis of self-representations and early object representations may prove more readily accessible to emotional uncovering and working through by increased emphasis on the careful and persistent analysis of the total masturbatory process. This is not meant to imply that such analysis of the total masturbatory process is a substitute for the usual analysis of the patient's defenses,

310

symptoms, behavior, dreams, and transference relationships. Excerpts from the analyses of a number of patients have been presented to illustrate the therapeutic value of such emphasis, along with some attempt to indicate some of the vicissitudes of the total masturbatory process.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

What Analytic Work Requires of an Analyst—A Job Analysis

Joan Fleming M.D.

Joan Fleming

Joan Fleming  

Selection of candidates for psychoanalytic training is a constant task for our training facilities and a constant challenge. Each year decisions are made to accept some applicants and to reject others. Much time and effort is spent on coming to these decisions and since the training program itself is long and costly, mistakes become expensive for all concerned.

To avoid mistakes and their consequent waste would be reason enough to warrant close scrutiny of the problems inherent in selection. But there is another equally cogent reason, namely, to arrive, if possible, at criteria for selection which go beyond the impressionistic "sizing-up" so commonly used as a basis for accepting or rejecting an applicant. We need to define in more precise and more communicable terms our reasons for selection decisions. Systematic studies of the problems which the task of selection presents and of the procedures used to study them need to be undertaken.

The process of selection is a process of prediction. Each selection choice is one which makes a prediction regarding the eventual success of the applicant as an analyst. This is a judgment that predicts success or failure in a particular task. A systematic study of the process of making such predictions requires a knowledge of what the particular task demands. A necessary step in this direction is an analysis of the activity for which success or failure is being predicted. In the language of the many investigators making predictive studies (8), (9), (17), it is called a job analysis.

This paper presents an attempt at a job analysis. It tries to answer the question what are we selecting for.

Submitted November 4, 1960

719

[[RunningHead]]

The Selection of Candidates for Psychoanalytic Training11

Ralph Greenson M.D.

Ralph Greenson

Ralph Greenson  

In her brief introductory remarks, Joan Fleming emphasized the need for systematic study of the problems encountered in the selection of psychoanalytic candidates. Decisions for admission to training are based on assessments of undeveloped potentials and therefore are predictive judgments compared to assessments of performance as an analyst after a period of training. A systematic study must therefore turn to the methods used in prediction studies in general.

In a systematic study of this sort, there are two basic questions to be answered. (1) What does the work of analysis require that would call for special talents and skills in the analyst? (2) How can we recognize at the stage of admission and evaluate at that time the capacities for the development of these special talents and skills in an applicant? The first question requiries a "job analysis" for the definition of the qualities to be developed. The second question calls for a study of instruments and procedures which will measure the potentialities present in the applicant.

This panel was planned to provide a review of the literature on the subject of selection of psychoanalytic candidates; a description of a "job analysis"; some ideas on the work of the selector as an instrument in the predictive process; and a paper on the process of evaluation in the individual interview. This was followed by a report of some of the findings in Columbia's ten-year systematic study of selection.

The first formal presentation was by George H. Pollock, who spoke on "Historical Perspectives in the Selection of Candidates for Psychoanalytic Training." He outlined the important steps and changes which have occurred in the training of psychoanalysts, beginning with Freud's first remarks in 1902 and proceeding to the present day. It was a fascinating experience to make the journey from discussions of training which consisted of two weekly after-dinner walks with Freud, to the comprehensive and demanding minimal standards which are now operative in the American Psychoanalytic Association. In this chronological account, several events stand out as turning points.

[[RunningHead]]

Research in Psychoanalysis11

Arnold Pfeffer M.D.

Arnold Pfeffer

Arnold Pfeffer  

This panel concerned itself with the question of whether or not, and how, scientific progress in psychoanalysis may be facilitated through planned, systematic research.

As suggested by Robert Waelder, the panel pursued these questions with the assumption that basic analytic concepts are sufficiently and satisfactorily demonstrated by analysis itself. The focus of the panel was on those question that arise from analysis itself but in their investigation perhaps require research strategies and devices in addition to those of analysis itself.

These issues were discussed on the basis of specific examples of promising, ongoing research projects: a study of pregnancy; a study of twins; and a study of "giftedness." The discussion of these projects then led to more general theoretical considerations of research in psychoanalysis.

In a paper entitled "The Role of Designed Research in Psychoanalysis, " Grete L. Bibring raised the question of clinical evidence, that is, how objective is the basis on which the analyst formulates new theoretical ideas or new ways to look at well-known pieces of clinical theory; also in the communication of such new ideas to other analysts, how objective are the bases for their acceptance or rejection; and, finally, are there more suitable ways in which we can study more directly the psychoanalytic observations behind new hypotheses, applying methods which are designed differently from those ordinarily used by the analyst and coming closer to providing the opportunity to validate our clinical findings and test our assumptions?

Using as an example a particular hypothesis relating to the mother-infant relationship, Bibring suggested a specific research design with which to test this hypothesis. The idea she wishes to test is that there is a compelling force for a mother to recapitulate with her own children intense and unsolved conflicts that as a daughter she had had with her own mother. The question is whether this daughter-mother conflict has such an overriding impact and bearing on the daughter's attitude toward her own baby that it stands out as central and crucial among the other infantile conflicts and object relationships which affect the mother-child attitude.

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Theory of the Symbolic Process11

Nathan Segel M.D.

Nathan Segel

Nathan Segel  

Samuel A. Guttman opened the panel with a presentation entitled "Comments on the Symbol and the Symbolic Process." Cognizance was first taken of the fact that most of the panel members had shared a common experience in being stimulated to reformulate their conceptions of symbols as a result of participating in Brenner's Section of the Kris Study Group. Guttman then developed what was essentially a panoramic view of the subject, indicating both its scope and a frame of reference within the existing body of psychoanalytic theory and knowledge. The range of the symbol and the symbolic process were traced from their use by primitive man through their application in the highest forms of artistic creativity. Using as a starting point modern art, with its creation and communication of feeling through its use of form and color, he contrasted its frequent absence of "manifest significance" with the "conceptualized symbols of the Greeks" and the "magical symbols of the prehistoric man." The latter was the adaptive response of primitive man in his attempt to cope with phenomena he could not otherwise perceive or master; to attain a feeling of familiarity with ideas like death, puzzling natural events, and intense inner strivings. Hence important aspects of his internal and external economy were domesticated by means of symbol formation. In the course of development the symbols became useful as a means of communication and even later as tools for expressing thoughts, ideas, and feelings of increasing complexity. The earliest symbols were zoomorphic, and later became anthropomorphic. For early man the symbol was reality. "It portrayed reality before it came to pass." During the days of Greek civilization the symbol had become an abstract concept that stemmed from the word symbalon, meaning "the adjustment of various parts to form a whole, " the latter also including the idea of bringing "separates together." What is behind this capacity, and what are some of the important facets of this process, would be among the tasks of the panel. Although no attempt was made to present a formal summary of the literature on the subject, Guttman was struck by the paucity of papers primarily devoted to this subject. He, like others who followed him, emphasized the importance of Freud's contributions to the topic of symbolism, particularly those Freud made in Chapter VII of his Interpretation of Dreams (1900).

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychology of Twins11

Edward Joseph M.D.

Edward Joseph

Edward Joseph  

Jacob A. Arlow, in his introduction, commented upon the increased interest that has been shown by psychoanalysts in the past few years in studying the problems of identical twins. He pointed out that very few studies have been made using identical twins as experiments in nature, to determine various aspects of the influence of environment; even less work has been done on the special psychology of the individuals who comprise a pair of identical twins. Such studies, he said, might be expected to throw light on aspects of developmental psychology, character formation, and symptom formation, as well as upon the subjective experience of having a twin and being a twin. It was toward this latter problem that the panel would devote itself first, turning then to the methodology of twin studies and the problems of twin research.

Arlow presented a paper based upon the study of two identical twins, each the member of a different twin pair, whom he had analyzed. The first of these twins had been the younger of the pair; his sibling had died when the twins were in their late teens. To this loss he reacted by a massive denial, manifested as follows: he would say goodnight to the brother's empty bed, would talk to his brother while walking, and visit his grave to speak to him. He conceived the fantasy of having taken his brother inside himself. Thus his twin was not dead but remained alive within him. This internalized Dybbuk caused him intense suffering, but as long as he experienced such suffering, he was able to maintain denial of his brother's death. The brothers had had friendly relations with each other, the patient being the younger, more submissive, and more admiring of the pair. After the denial had been analyzed it was possible to see that there was much hostility in the patient toward his older brother, centering about the desire to have been, not a twin, but a single individual. There were regressive fantasies of intrauterine existence as a single individual, of being born alone, and of securing all of mother's nourishment for himself. Being the younger of the pair he felt guilty, since his birth had created the twin pair; he was an intruder, who had in effect robbed the older brother of his birthright.

[[RunningHead]]

Some Developmental Observations Relating to the Theory of Anxiety11

John Benjamin M.D.

John Benjamin

John Benjamin  

It is not my intention in this presentation to review and discuss the psychoanaltyic theory of anxiety as a whole. Rather, I wish to bring a few data from the first year or so of life which have some pertinence to this theory, and to developmental theory in general. In so doing, I shall assume familiarity with most of the many recent discussions of anxiety in the psychoanalytic literature, such as those of Rangell (42), Greenacre (27), (28), Schur (47), (48), Spitz (52), (53), (54), Zetzel (60), Flescher (16), Kubie (35), and Brenner (5), to mention just a few.

Even with this major restriction of goal, I have had to make a choice between two possible modes of presentation. The first would be a systematic exposition of methods and data in one specific and delimited area; the second a necessarily incomplete and inadequate coverage of larger segments of the developmental process as they relate to anxiety. Had I chosen the first, I would be devoting the whole of this paper to such a topic, for example, as the differentiation between stranger anxiety and separation anxiety in the infant and young child, instead of including it as one of a series of subjects to be touched upon briefly. In choosing the second mode, as being more appropriate to this panel, I find myself in the position of reversing the ordinary and in principle better procedure of presenting well-established data before drawing theoretical conclusions from them.

Submitted September 3, 1959

[[RunningHead]]

Some Early Prototypes of Ego Defenses11

René Spitz M.D.

René Spitz

René Spitz  

Conflict and defense are among the most fundamental and important concepts introduced by Freud in his earliest psychoanalytic publications. He mentioned them already in the letters to Fliess; they remain the focus of our attention to this day. Freud continued refining these concepts and, speaking of the instinct being turned back on the ego and of the instinct undergoing reversal from activity to passivity, he remarked: "Perhaps they represent attempts at defense which, at higher stages of the development of the ego, are effected by other means" (15).

Here a development of the defenses from a more primitive to a more organized stage is clearly suggested. Subsequently this suggestion was taken up by other authors, beginning with Anna Freud's discussion of the chronological sequence of defense mechanisms (9). In 1939, Hartmann (22) started exploring some of the early physiological prototypes for later psychological defenses, a question taken up more recently by Menninger (29) and Greenacre (20).

It is the purpose of this paper to apply the genetic principle to some of the defense mechanisms of the ego in an attempt to uncover some of their physiological prototypes. In this attempt I will consider phenomena of neonate behavior and functioning, both perceptual and neurophysiological. I have selected them because their mode of functioning seems to present sufficient analogies with the mode of functioning of specific later defense mechanisms to warrant investigation. In some cases, I will be able to point out certain transitional features.

Submitted September 29, 1959

[[RunningHead]]

Frigidity in Women11

Burness Moore M.D.

Burness Moore

Burness Moore  

Despite the long-standing theoretical and clinical interest of psychoanalysts in female sexuality, the panel discussion summarized in this report was the first of this Association to consider specifically the problem of frigidity. It was appropriately organized under the chairmanship of Helene Deutsch, who chose in her introduction to take a stand based on her many years of work in female psychology. Because her remarks provide a succinct statement of the considered viewpoint of one who is the respected dean of this field of psychoanalytic investigation, more attention will be given to them in this report than is usually devoted to the chairman's orientation of a panel.

Helene Deutsch said that after the publication of her two volumes on female psychology in 1944, consultations, short-term therapy, and other contacts with the subject had given her an opportunity for a macroscopic review of the psychic events and psychopathology which she had previously seen microscopically in analysis. She was shocked by the high incidence of so-called frigidity in women and disappointed in the results of psychoanalytic treatment for it. Cases of very severe neurotic illness had been helped without eliminating this problem. She saw psychotic women and aggressive, masculine women who experienced intense vaginal orgasm, while loving, giving, maternal, and happy women did not, even though they felt fully gratified.

Many conditions, she said, have to be fulfilled before a woman reaches orgastic capacity: (1) influences of pregenital and preoedipal developments have to be conquered; (2) oedipal problems have to be solved; (3) bisexuality has to be resolved; (4) the main source of sexual inhibition, feminine masochism, has to be under full control; (5) object relations must be on a postambivalent basis. Not only may disturbances in every single phase of development become a source of frigidity, but so may the psychological meaning of reproductive functions themselves, including coitus and the first act of this function. Intercourse and motherhood mobilize a struggle between the narcissistic elements of self-preservation and the object-directed demands of reproduction which constitute a danger for the security and solidity of the ego. She expressed her agreement with Therese Benedek who sees in frigidity a defense against these dangers.

[[RunningHead]]

Silence and the Theory of Technique

Jacob Arlow M.D.

Jacob Arlow

Jacob Arlow  

SUMMARY

In the complex setting of the treatment situation no set of rules may be promulgated to meet each specific occasion when the patient stops talking. Nothing can replace the sensitivity of the therapist nor his empathic response to the unfolding concatenation of influences at any moment in the transference. However, a careful review of the events antecedent to the silence and their proper assessment from the point of view of which functional relationship of the ego is primarily involved may be useful. The therapist's interpretation is an intervention intended to alter the relationship of forces between the discharge tendencies of the id or superego on one hand and the defensive function of the ego on the other. An evaluation of the conflict which led to the interruption of communication must be made in structural terms. On the basis of such an analysis the therapist may decide whether the intervention should emphasize the fending off or the discharge tendencies implicit in the silence. The difference in therapeutic effectiveness between interpretations of the unconscious wish versus interpretations of defense and the importance of appropriate timing of such interventions have been stressed by several authors, notably Loewenstein (8), (9) and Lewin (7). Loewenstein points out, for example, that in the situation in which the ego has instituted a certain defense in order to ward off anxiety associated with an emergent id or superego wish, an interpretation directed at exposing the strength of the wish will lead to a reinforcement of the defensive effort of the ego. Analogously, in our present context, such interpretations would result in an intensification—or, more accurately—a prolongation of the silence. Similarly, Lewin's observations on the relationship between analytic technique, hypnosis, and sleep are apropos at this point. An untimely awareness by the patient of his warded-off wishes may stop the flow of associations in the same manner that the terrifying undisguised dream images may affect the process of dreaming. The dreamer interrupts the process of producing dream images by breaking off both dream and sleep. The patient breaks off the flow of free associations and the permitted regression which it implies by lapsing into

53

silence. Interpretative efforts directed at the opposing set of forces, namely, the defensive endeavors of the ego in such situations, have an opposite effect, comparable to the effect of an efficiently functioning dream censorship which permits the subject to sleep and dream. Such interpretations provide the necessary assurance that the defensive effort is still working efficiently in order to permit the patient to continue to talk and associate freely.

Particular attention must be given to the total configuration of the silence in the transference, i.e., whether a specific pattern or significant event is being reproduced. In such instances, a silence may represent a classic expression of transference, a communication concerning experience in which silence was an essential component of the event. An interpretation which can correlate such a silence with a specific relationship or to some precise event in the individual's past is usually most effective. Finally, there are those silences of patients which must be respected, silences which represent a period during which the patient struggles to maintain control over his feelings or to re-establish a sense of self-esteem after some recollection by the patient or some intervention by the therapist which may have wrought violence to the patient's narcissism. Special patterns of silences such as those brief interruptions of communication which serve as isolating barriers between two different trends of thought, naturally, must be treated as specific manifestations of the defensive process in their proper context.

[[RunningHead]]

The Silent Patient—Introduction11

Rudolph Loewenstein M.D.

Rudolph Loewenstein

Rudolph Loewenstein  

It is a great pleasure and privilege to chair a panel composed of such distinguished members of the American Psychoanalytic Association—a panel, moreover, on a subject of such fascinating interest and importance as the one we shall discuss today.

The psychoanalytic process reflects the pre-existing neurotic process, inasmuch as it forms a continuation of it and achieves in favorable cases to modify its outcome. Everything that happens to the patient in analysis is under the simultaneous sway of two apparently opposite forces. One moves him to communicate the data relevant to his neurosis, while the other compels him to resist such communication. Both are constantly operative, but mixed in varying proportions. It is only in some extreme, exceptional moments that one or the other force alone seems to be at work. Indeed, even when the patient's resistance appears maximized, when he refuses to communicate or even is ready to interrupt his treatment, the very motives of his resistance reveal the nature of his neurotic conflict and thus must be understood as a communication—by repetition—of the warded-off conflict. And even in moments when the resistance seems at a minimum, when insight is being gained during what Kris (2) called "the good psychoanalytic hour, " this insight actually integrates the interplay between drives and defenses. Indeed, without resistance the psychoanalytic process is not possible.

Submitted June 10, 1959

1Panel held at the Midwinter Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, December 6, 1958.2

[[RunningHead]]

On the Silence and Sounds of the Analytic Hour

Ralph Greenson M.D.

Ralph Greenson

Ralph Greenson  

I

This contribution to the panel on the silent patient is essentially a clinical one. I should like to begin by stressing the importance of differentiating between two large categories of silence: silence as a resistance, and silence as a communication. The most frequent silence met with in psychoanalytic practice is the silence of resistance (1). This silence means that the patient is either consciously or unconsciously unwilling to verbalize. Since the patients in our psychoanalytic practice are attempting to communicate to us in accordance with the basic rule, i.e., attempting to put all their thoughts into words, if they become silent, it means that they are opposing the procedure of psychoanalysis. It is then our task to overcome this obstacle by attempting to find the motives for this resistance. Here, we are often aided in our task by the fact that the patient communicates despite his resistance (4). The patient may betray either the motive or even the content of his resistance by his posture, his movements, or his facial expression (7).

For example: a patient who had been talking now falls silent. Her face which had been turned so that it was visible to the analyst now is averted. Her hands which had been lying calmly and openly at her side become fidgety. She plays with her ring and then covers her breasts with her arms. Her legs are now tightly crossed at the ankles. Her eyes are downcast; her lips twitch nervously; her face is flushed. She squirms uncomfortably on the analytic couch. It appears that the patient is embarrassed.

Submitted June 12, 1959

79
